title: Jim Barry Wines appoints first female senior winemaker in 67-year history
summary:
Jim Barry Wines has announced the appointment of Topsi Wallace as its senior winemaker, making her the first female to hold the position in its 67 years of operation. The winery said that the appointment recognises her six years of “outstanding contribution” to the family-owned winery and the “pivotal role” she has played across all aspects of winemaking. Jim Barry Wines explained that she consistently demonstrates exceptional technical skill, leadership, sound judgement, and a deep understanding of the brand’s philosophy. Director of winemaking Tom Barry said that Wallace has “more than earned this role”. “Her work ethic, attention to detail, and ability to lead with confidence and clarity have been evident for years. This appointment is a natural progression based on merit, experience, and results,” he said.

The post Jim Barry Wines appoints first female senior winemaker in 67-year history appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Newly appointed senior winemaker, Topsi Wallace. Image supplied

Jim Barry Wines has announced the appointment of Topsi Wallace as its senior winemaker, making her the first female to hold the position in its 67 years of operation.

The winery said that the appointment recognises her six years of “outstanding contribution” to the family-owned winery and the “pivotal role” she has played across all aspects of winemaking.

Jim Barry Wines explained that she consistently demonstrates exceptional technical skill, leadership, sound judgement, and a deep understanding of the brand’s philosophy.

Director of winemaking Tom Barry said that Wallace has “more than earned this role”.

“Her work ethic, attention to detail, and ability to lead with confidence and clarity have been evident for years. This appointment is a natural progression based on merit, experience, and results,” he said.

Although the appointment of its first female senior winemaker is a milestone, Jim Barry Wines emphasised that the outcome is “of proven capability and sustained performance”.

“Topsi’s role has been earned through hard work, expertise, and leadership. She has our complete trust and respect,” said Barry.

In the last four years, Jim Barry Wines has achieved 206 gold medals and 64 trophies at Australian Wine Shows, making it the most awarded Riesling and Shiraz producer in the country.

“Topsi has played a central role in achieving these outstanding results, of which we are incredibly proud.”

Jim Barry Wines said that senior winemaker is the highest winemaking position at Jim Barry Wines, outside of the Barry family, and “carries responsibility for overseeing production, upholding quality standards across the portfolio, and mentoring the wider winemaking team”.

Originally from New Zealand’s South Island, Wallace holds a Bachelor of Viticulture and Oenology from Lincoln University. She relocated permanently to Australia in 2019 and has completed 11 Australian vintages, alongside Northern Hemisphere experience in Canada and France, as well as vintages in her native New Zealand. She completed a Diploma of Business Studies through Massey University in 2022, the Australian Wine Industry Advanced Course (AWAC) in 2024, and is currently undertaking her WSET Diploma, having completed WSET Level 3 in 2025.

Wallace is actively involved in wine judging across Australia, including having served at the Adelaide Royal and Melbourne Royal Wine Shows as an associate judge. Moreover, she is a long-standing member of the Clare Valley Wine Show Committee.

title: Coonawarra tutorials set to ‘dig deep’ in Adelaide and Brisbane
summary:
The Coonawarra Vignerons has revealed that it will be bringing its ‘Coonawarra Tutorial: Digging Deeper than the Dirt’ to Adelaide and Brisbane this June following last year’s success in Melbourne and Sydney. The tutorial aims to showcase the past, present and future of the wine region “going beyond the soil” to explore viticulture and the evolving styles of Coonawarra wines. The events will take place in Adelaide on Wednesday 3 June and Brisbane Wednesday 10 June, “bringing together leading sommeliers, wine trade, and media for an engaging and educational tasting experience”. “It’s time to relook at the wines from the region, recognising the 131-year-old past but embracing the subtle changes that are being made across the region to make Coonawarra more relevant than ever,” said Dan Redman, fourth-generation winemaker and president of Coonawarra Vignerons.

The post Coonawarra tutorials set to ‘dig deep’ in Adelaide and Brisbane appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Last year’s tutorials in Melbourne (pictured) and Sydney were a success. Image supplied

The Coonawarra Vignerons has revealed that it will be bringing its ‘Coonawarra Tutorial: Digging Deeper than the Dirt’ to Adelaide and Brisbane this June following last year’s success in Melbourne and Sydney.

The tutorial aims to showcase the past, present and future of the wine region “going beyond the soil” to explore viticulture and the evolving styles of Coonawarra wines.

The events will take place in Adelaide on Wednesday 3 June and Brisbane Wednesday 10 June, “bringing together leading sommeliers, wine trade, and media for an engaging and educational tasting experience”.

A select group of representatives will be given the opportunity to hear panel members Dan Redman (Redman Wines), Matilda Innes (Ottelia), Maddie LeMire (Balnaves of Coonawarra) and Ben Harris (Wynns Coonawarra Estate), present a range of wines dating back to the 1960s with discussion facilitated by wine educator Sarah Andrew (DipWSET Honours).

“It’s time to relook at the wines from the region, recognising the 131-year-old past but embracing the subtle changes that are being made across the region to make Coonawarra more relevant than ever,” said Dan Redman, fourth-generation winemaker and president of Coonawarra Vignerons.

With a population of just 135 people, the organisation said the region’s strength lies in “community spirit, collaboration, innovation, and a shared vision to keep Coonawarra wines relevant for future generations”.

Coonawarra Vignerons said that the tutorials would not be possible without funding and support from the South Australian Government through the Department of Primary Industries and Regions.

There are limited places available for both the Adelaide and Brisbane tutorials, with interested parties recommended to contact Emma Weir via her email address below:

emma@mastermindconsulting.com.au

title: Entries open for milestone 200th Sydney Royal Wine Show and industry symposium
summary:
The Royal Agricultural Society of NSW (RAS) invites Australian wine producers to enter the Sydney Royal Wine Show for its 200th anniversary. Throughout its history, the RAS said that the show has been guided by some of Australian wine’s greatest figures, including Len Evans AO, James Halliday AM, Brian Croser AO, Iain Riggs AM, Samantha Connew, PJ Charteris and Sarah Crowe. This year’s show will be led by chair of judges Mike de Iuliis, who is joined by international judge Matthew Jukes. The RAS emphasised that a Sydney Royal medal is “one of the most recognised and trusted marks in Australian wine”, with a milestone year such as this one carrying “more weight than ever”.

The post Entries open for milestone 200th Sydney Royal Wine Show and industry symposium appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Sydney Royal Wine Show chair of judges Mike de Iuliis. Image supplied

The Royal Agricultural Society of NSW (RAS) invites Australian wine producers to enter the Sydney Royal Wine Show for its 200th anniversary.

Throughout its history, the RAS said that the show has been guided by some of Australian wine’s greatest figures, including Len Evans AO, James Halliday AM, Brian Croser AO, Iain Riggs AM, Samantha Connew, PJ Charteris and Sarah Crowe.

This year’s show will be led by chair of judges Mike de Iuliis, who is joined by international judge Matthew Jukes. The RAS emphasised that a Sydney Royal medal is “one of the most recognised and trusted marks in Australian wine”, with a milestone year such as this one carrying “more weight than ever”.

Chair of wine committee Angus Barnes said that the show has been setting the benchmark for Australian wine quality since 1826.

“The show has kept pace with every shift in Australian wine — from fortified styles to the classic varietal wines Australia is celebrated for globally, through to the emerging styles reflecting how Australian drinking continues to evolve.

“With nearly 2,000 wines entered on average each year, being part of Sydney Royal means being part of the institution that has helped showcase the best of Australian wine to the world for two centuries,” Barnes said.

Entries cost $150 per exhibit and close 20 May.

The RAS is also proud to announce an industry symposium titled the ‘Sydney Royal 200: The Wine Show Conversation’, with priority access for tickets to be given to Sydney Royal Wine Show entrants.

The symposium will immediately follow judging on 31 July, with 200 places available for wine professionals “to explore the Australian wine show system and go behind the scenes to see how wine is assessed”. RAS said the day will be full of tasting, debate and celebration of Australian wine. General ticket sales opened last Friday.

“Over 200 years it has been a constant theme of cherishing all that is great about the past, while always having the future front of mind, embracing innovation, fostering youth and nurturing communities as we move collectively into the next generation,” said Sally Evans, Wine committee member and RAS councillor.

content:
2025 Young Viticulturist of the Year finalists. Image supplied

New Zealand Wine has revealed that young viticulturists around the country are turning their focus to the 2026 Ormond Nurseries New Zealand Young Viticulturist of the Year awards.

The competition has been running for twenty years, “supporting over 250 Young Viticulturists, with many coming back year after year, such is their passion and determination to win the prestigious title”.

Nicky Grandorge, leadership and development manager at New Zealand Winegrowers, said there is always excitement as the new Young Viticulturist competition season begins.

“The contestants, sponsors and organisers are all raring to go, and there is such a buzz as everyone comes together to support the future viticultural leaders of New Zealand,” she said.

This year’s regional competitions are set to run from 28 May to 2 July before the national final at Escarpment in Martinborough on 26 August.

The competition dates are as follows:

28 May – Marlborough

4 June – North Island Regional, Wairarapa

11 June – Hawke’s Bay

18 June – South Island Regional, North Canterbury

2 July – Central Otago, Cromwell

The 2025 Young Viticulturist of the Year was Anna Kelland from Constellation Brands in Marlborough.

New Zealand Winegrowers said that the competition is made possible by sponsorships from itself as well as Ormond Nurseries, BioStart, Ecotrellis, Syngenta Biologicals, Constellation Brands, Empak, Fendt, Fruitfed Supplies, Indevin, Klima, Lowe, Waterforce, and The New Zealand Society for Viticulture and Oenology (NZSVO).

title: Bleasdale triumphs at 2026 Langhorne Creek Wine Show
summary:
Langhorne Creek grapegrowers, wineries, trade, suppliers and representatives from local and state government agencies came together last Friday to celebrate the 2026 Langhorne Creek Wine Show. Bleasdale winery saw much success at the ceremony, winning the ‘Giles Machinery Best Malbec’ trophy and the ‘Multi-Color Corporation Champion Wine of Show’ award for its Bleasdale Reed Patch 2025 Single Vineyard Malbec before being announced as the ‘Vinpac Producer of the Year’. Chair of judges Adam Walls (Wine Selectors) was joined by judges Alexandra Alavoine (Treasury Wine Estates) and wine communicator Samantha Payne, as well as associate judges Charlotte Hardy (Charlotte Dalton Wines) and Gonzalo Sanchez (Lloyd Brothers). In his first year as chair, Walls was impressed by Langhorne Creek’s diversity.

The post Bleasdale triumphs at 2026 Langhorne Creek Wine Show appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Nicole Brasier, Bleasdale senior winemaker. Image supplied

Langhorne Creek grapegrowers, wineries, trade, suppliers and representatives from local and state government agencies came together last Friday to celebrate the 2026 Langhorne Creek Wine Show.

Bleasdale winery saw much success at the ceremony, winning the ‘Giles Machinery Best Malbec’ trophy and the ‘Multi-Color Corporation Champion Wine of Show’ award for its Bleasdale Reed Patch 2025 Single Vineyard Malbec before being announced as the ‘Vinpac Producer of the Year’.

Watkins Wine vineyard manager Mark Evans was named the ‘Peats Soil & Garden Supplies Grape Grower of the Year’ for “his longstanding contribution to the Langhorne Creek region and community”.

Other awards saw a variety of producers acknowledged, with trophies going to the Bremerton 2025 Special Release Fiano, the W Salter & Son 2025 Pepperjack Grenache Rosé, the Wolf Blass 2024 Grey Label Cabernet Shiraz, the Lake Breeze Wines 2024 Cabernet Sauvignon, the Kimbolton 2021 The Rifleman Shiraz, the Lake Breeze 2025 Nero D’Avola and the Lake Breeze Wines 2016 Arthur’s Reserve Cabernet Sauvignon.

Chair of judges Adam Walls (Wine Selectors) was joined by judges Alexandra Alavoine (Treasury Wine Estates) and wine communicator Samantha Payne, as well as associate judges Charlotte Hardy (Charlotte Dalton Wines) and Gonzalo Sanchez (Lloyd Brothers).

In his first year as chair, Walls was impressed by Langhorne Creek’s diversity.

“The show was an insightful immersion into the region’s wines, with an impressively high standard of winemaking demonstrated across all classes.

“The red categories revealed an encouraging trend toward fresher, more vibrant expressions, complemented by thoughtful and well-integrated oak usage.

“The alternative red and white classes further highlighted the region’s versatility, showcasing that its strengths extend well beyond the traditional stalwarts of Cabernet Sauvignon and Shiraz.

“The Malbec class was especially noteworthy. The 20 wines presented demonstrated remarkable diversity in vineyard origin, clonal selection and house style, reinforcing the view that Langhorne Creek comfortably sits at the top of this variety’s totem pole,” he said.

Walls awarded the ‘Chair of Judges trophy’ to the Terrachroma Wines 2025 Long Shot Greco.

“Not only was this wine aromatic and full of flavour, [but] it also had textural weight and chew. The role of texture and mouthfeel in Australian white wine has become more considered of late, more discussed and celebrated. This wine perfectly captured our maturity in how we look at white wines.”

The full list of trophy winners is available below:

  • Best White Wine, sponsored by Langhorne Creek Grape & Wine Inc – Bremerton 2025 Special Release Fiano

 

  • Best Rosé or Sparkling Wine, sponsored by Peats Soil & Garden Supplies – W Salter & Son 2025 Pepperjack Grenache Rosé

 

  • Best Blended Red Wine, sponsored by Cooperages 1912 – Wolf Blass Wines 2024 Grey Label Langhorne Creek Cabernet Shiraz

 

  • Best Cabernet Sauvignon, sponsored by FrostBoss – Lake Breeze Wines 2024 Cabernet Sauvignon

 

  • Best Shiraz, sponsored by Project Wine Pty Ltd – Kimbolton 2021 The Rifleman Shiraz

 

  • Best Malbec, sponsored by Giles Machinery – Bleasdale Reed Patch 2025 Single Vineyard Malbec

 

  • Best Red from Alternative or Other Varieties, sponsored by Jon Burke Creative Digital Video – Lake Breeze 2025 Nero D’avola

 

  • Best Museum or Fortified Wine, sponsored by Daish Irrigation & Netafim – Lake Breeze Wines 2016 Arthur’s Reserve Cabernet Sauvignon

 

  • Chair of Judges award – Terrachroma Wines 2025 Long Shot Greco

 

  • Champion Wine of the Show, sponsored by Multi-Color Corporation – Bleasdale Reed Patch 2025 Single Vineyard Malbec

 

  • Producer of the Year, sponsored by Vinpac – Bleasdale Vineyards

 

  • Grape Grower of the Year, sponsored by Peats Soil & Garden Supplies – Mark Evans

title: Penfolds commemorates 40 years of wine guide with 9th edition
summary:
Penfolds has released the 9th edition of its Rewards of Patience back-vintage wine guide, marking 40 years “of one of the most respected wine publications in the world”. Since 1985, Penfolds has opened its cellars to an invited panel of independent, highly acclaimed wine critics from around the globe to taste and review its archive of wines. The insights, tasting notes and perspectives of these critics is the published into Rewards of Patience alongside the latest research and the stories behind each Penfolds wine. Compiled and chronicled by independent author Andrew Caillard MW, the book is affectionately known as the Penfolds “Form Guide” and “widely regarded as the definitive reference for all things Penfolds”.

The post Penfolds commemorates 40 years of wine guide with 9th edition appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
17 wine critics from Hong Kong, London, Paris and Adelaide contributed to the guide. Image supplied

Penfolds has released the 9th edition of its Rewards of Patience back-vintage wine guide, marking 40 years “of one of the most respected wine publications in the world”.

Since 1985, Penfolds has opened its cellars to an invited panel of independent, highly acclaimed wine critics from around the globe to taste and review its archive of wines. The insights, tasting notes and perspectives of these critics is the published into Rewards of Patience alongside the latest research and the stories behind each Penfolds wine.

Compiled and chronicled by independent author Andrew Caillard MW, the book is affectionately known as the Penfolds “Form Guide” and “widely regarded as the definitive reference for all things Penfolds”.

Over a six-month period, a panel of 17 wine critics came together across Hong Kong, London, Paris and Adelaide to taste more than 500 wines from Penfolds Museum. Grange, Bin 707 and Yattarna led the tasting, however, the panel were also shown some of the winery’s “most rare and limited releases”, including Bin 60A Coonawarra Cabernet, Kalimna Vineyard Block 42 Cabernet Sauvignon and the Penfolds g Series.

“People from all walks of life have followed Penfolds for generations. It is one of the most collected Australian wine brands in the world and has a storied track record in the secondary markets. A complete collection of Grange and rare bottlings can command eye-watering auction prices, while regular trading in more recent vintages highlights its popularity and dependability.

“In addition, it is one of the most enjoyable, versatile and trusted wine styles in the world, especially with a few years or more of bottle age. Hence why Penfolds appeals to such a varied audience, in so many countries,” author Caillard said.

Chief winemaker for Penfolds Peter Gago AC, said that each edition of the wine guide “ascends to a new level”.

“As wine drinkers and collectors, we all need guidance and reference. This tome supports our search, and answers many of our questions – honestly, and as accurately as can be expected with ever-morphing wines and their subjective and emotive appreciation. It’s certainly an honour for the Penfolds winemaking team to remain intimately involved in such a project and such a pursuit,” he said.

The winery said that in addition to Penfolds Australian wines, the 9th edition also spotlights the brand’s expanding international portfolio, including wines from California, France and China with over 500 tasting notes inside the book.

The book is available for $50 RRP as a hardback copy or $29 for a paperback via the Penfolds website.

title: Four Winds Vineyard makes Australia’s first ‘circular wine’ using black soldier fly larvae
summary:
Four Winds Vineyard has released Australia’s first ‘circular wine’ in collaboration with hotelier Hyatt Regency Sydney and food waste innovator Goterra using millions of fly larvae. The initiative sees food waste from the Hyatt Regency consumed by the larvae and recycled onsite into a rich frass, or fertiliser, which nourishes the grapevines that are used to produce Four Winds Vineyard’s new ‘The Circular Vintage’ series. “In the circular production process, organic waste from the Hyatt’s kitchens goes directly into Goterra’s specially built Modular Infrastructure for Biological Services (MIB) unit in the hotel’s basement. Resembling a shipping container the size of a single car space, the MIB is filled with millions of black soldier fly larvae that eat their way through the food scraps, excreting nutrient-rich frass,” Four Winds Vineyard explained.

The post Four Winds Vineyard makes Australia’s first ‘circular wine’ using black soldier fly larvae appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The ‘frass’ used to nourish the grapevines. Image supplied

Four Winds Vineyard has released Australia’s first ‘circular wine’ in collaboration with hotelier Hyatt Regency Sydney and food waste innovator Goterra using millions of fly larvae.

The initiative sees food waste from the Hyatt Regency consumed by the larvae and recycled onsite into a rich frass, or fertiliser, which nourishes the grapevines that are used to produce Four Winds Vineyard’s new ‘The Circular Vintage’ series.

“In the circular production process, organic waste from the Hyatt’s kitchens goes directly into Goterra’s specially built Modular Infrastructure for Biological Services (MIB) unit in the hotel’s basement. Resembling a shipping container the size of a single car space, the MIB is filled with millions of black soldier fly larvae that eat their way through the food scraps, excreting nutrient-rich frass.

“The fertiliser is used on Four Winds Vineyard’s vines while the maggots, having doubled their size daily during a seven-day feeding frenzy, are used as protein for poultry and pet food,” Four Winds Vineyard explained.

Four Winds Vineyard CEO Sarah Collingwood said the circular wine project made perfect sense for the family-run vineyard.

“We loved the idea of being able to return nutrients to the soil rather than having them end up in landfill.

“For us, this was about finding a practical way to close the loop between food, farming and wine, and we’re incredibly proud to put our name to The Circular Vintage series, which shows that innovation and quality can go hand in hand.

“The Circular Vintage Shiraz is a typical cool-climate Shiraz with distinctive white pepper and spice characters. We are equally excited about the Riesling, which has been hand-picked recently and has the delicate citrus and floral flavours you come to expect from a Canberra District Riesling,” Collingwood said.

Goterra CEO Olympia Yarger, who originally partnered with Hyatt Regency Sydney in 2024 to install the robot-controlled maggot farm as a way of sustainably managing the hotel’s food waste, said the beauty of the process is that nothing is wasted.

“This is the first time our system has been used to support wine production.

“Using insect frass to nourish vines creates a solution that benefits the vineyard, the hotel and the environment, and opens the door for new thinking across the wine industry,” she said.

Hyatt Regency Sydney executive assistant manager Nitin Kumar said Four Winds Vineyard stood out as a producer that shared the hotel’s commitment to quality and innovation.

“Partnering with Four Winds Vineyard on an Australia-first initiative like this allows us to showcase wines with a story that resonates well beyond the glass,” he said.

The Four Winds Vineyard 2025 Circular Vintage Shiraz is being poured for the first time today in the Hyatt Regency’s restaurant and lounge bar. A Circular Vintage Riesling will be introduced at the hotel in September.

content:

Casella Family Brands has announced a “new strategic distribution partnership” in New Zealand with Hancocks Family Merchants, effective 1 July 2026.

title: The Islander Estate Vineyards achieves sustainability certification across its operations
summary:
The Islander Estate Vineyards has received certification from Sustainable Winegrowing Australia (SWA), an achievement the winery said strengthens its commitment to environmentally conscious viticulture and winemaking. The new certification covers Islander Estate Vineyards’ four Kangaroo Island vineyards and winery operations. The estate said that it has farmed without synthetic pesticides, herbicides, growth regulators, or GMOs for 15 years. Yale Norris, co-owner of Islander Estate Vineyards, said that its isolated location and existing growing principals made the winery a natural candidate for the SWA certification. “We haven't had to make significant changes to the way we grow or make wine. The certification is our commitment to ongoing improvements in sustainability that are independently audited by SWA, giving our customers the reassurance that we are doing our utmost to protect both this land and the wines we produce from it,” he explained.

The post The Islander Estate Vineyards achieves sustainability certification across its operations appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The certification covers all four vineyards and winery operations. Image supplied

The Islander Estate Vineyards has received certification from Sustainable Winegrowing Australia (SWA), an achievement the winery said strengthens its commitment to environmentally conscious viticulture and winemaking.

The new certification covers The Islander Estate Vineyards’ four Kangaroo Island vineyards and winery operations. The estate said that it has farmed without synthetic pesticides, herbicides, growth regulators, or GMOs for 15 years.

Yale Norris, co-owner of The Islander Estate Vineyards, said that its isolated location and existing growing principals made the winery a natural candidate for the SWA certification.

“We haven’t had to make significant changes to the way we grow or make wine. The certification is our commitment to ongoing improvements in sustainability that are independently audited by SWA, giving our customers the reassurance that we are doing our utmost to protect both this land and the wines we produce from it,” he explained.

Norris said that the breadth of the SWA program is a key part of its appeal.

“We were attracted to SWA because while environmental management is a key pillar, it encompasses social and economic performance alongside environmental stewardship, and the program is about continuous improvement. It sets the vision for where we want to be, not just confirms where we already are.”

The winery explained that SWA is the national certification program for Australian grapegrowers and winemakers, backed by the Australian Wine Research Institute, Wine Australia, and Australian Grape & Wine.

Certification takes a “holistic view” of sustainability across six key pillars: Land & Soil, Water, People & Business, Biodiversity, Energy and Waste. It requires regular independent audits and a commitment to ongoing measurement and improvement.

title: Sevenhill names just its 10th chief winemaker as it marks 175 years
summary:
Sevenhill Cellars in the Clare Valley has appointed just its 10th chief winemaker and its third-non-Jesuit winemaker in its 175-year history. Ben Marx will be joining Sevenhill following significant winemaking roles at Knappstein and Jim Barry Wines. The Jesuits and the board of Sevenhill Cellars said Marx brings to the role “a deep understanding of Clare’s viticultural landscape, a firm grounding in its traditions and a clear vision for its stylistic evolution”. Marx expressed his excitement to be joining the team. “After seven years at Jim Barry’s I am more than excited to join the team at Sevenhill Cellars - a new challenge, a fruit resource second to none and a team committed to not only be around for another 175 years but also to make wine of global benchmark quality”.

The post Sevenhill names just its 10th chief winemaker as it marks 175 years appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Ben Marx, the newly appointed chief winemaker. Image supplied

Sevenhill Cellars in the Clare Valley has appointed just its 10th chief winemaker and its third-non-Jesuit winemaker in its 175-year history.

Ben Marx will be joining Sevenhill following significant winemaking roles at Knappstein and Jim Barry Wines. The Jesuits and the board of Sevenhill Cellars said Marx brings to the role “a deep understanding of Clare’s viticultural landscape, a firm grounding in its traditions and a clear vision for its stylistic evolution”.

Marx expressed his excitement to be joining the team.

“After seven years at Jim Barry’s I am more than excited to join the team at Sevenhill Cellars – a new challenge, a fruit resource second to none and a team committed to not only be around for another 175 years but also to make wine of global benchmark quality.

“With some major developments happening over the next few years and the commitment of the Jesuits, I can’t wait to get started,” he said.

“The Barry family, particularly my late grandparents, Jim and Nancy, has a long association with Sevenhill Cellars and the Jesuit community, with mutual support at the heart of that relationship in both winemaking and in faith. We wish Ben and the Sevenhill team all the best as they embark on this new chapter,” said Tom Barry of Jim Barry Wines.

Fr Brendan J Kelly SJ, Superior of Sevenhill from the Jesuits community, said that the mantra of the first Jesuit to Sevenhill was “courage, patience and hope”.

“We are very excited at the appointment of Ben Marx as chief winemaker, joining our general manager, Christian Fraser, and viticulturist, Craig Richards, forming a formidable team to continue the tradition at Sevenhill of quality wine for over 175 years,” he said.

Founded in 1851, Sevenhill is the Clare Valley’s foundational winery, and, for its first 150 years, winemaking was conducted by Jesuits.

content:
Newly appointed senior winemaker, Topsi Wallace. Image supplied

Jim Barry Wines has announced the appointment of Topsi Wallace as its senior winemaker, making her the first female to hold the position in its 67 years of operation.

The winery said that the appointment recognises her six years of “outstanding contribution” to the family-owned winery and the “pivotal role” she has played across all aspects of winemaking.

Jim Barry Wines explained that she consistently demonstrates exceptional technical skill, leadership, sound judgement, and a deep understanding of the brand’s philosophy.

Director of winemaking Tom Barry said that Wallace has “more than earned this role”.

“Her work ethic, attention to detail, and ability to lead with confidence and clarity have been evident for years. This appointment is a natural progression based on merit, experience, and results,” he said.

Although the appointment of its first female senior winemaker is a milestone, Jim Barry Wines emphasised that the outcome is “of proven capability and sustained performance”.

“Topsi’s role has been earned through hard work, expertise, and leadership. She has our complete trust and respect,” said Barry.

In the last four years, Jim Barry Wines has achieved 206 gold medals and 64 trophies at Australian Wine Shows, making it the most awarded Riesling and Shiraz producer in the country.

“Topsi has played a central role in achieving these outstanding results, of which we are incredibly proud.”

Jim Barry Wines said that senior winemaker is the highest winemaking position at Jim Barry Wines, outside of the Barry family, and “carries responsibility for overseeing production, upholding quality standards across the portfolio, and mentoring the wider winemaking team”.

Originally from New Zealand’s South Island, Wallace holds a Bachelor of Viticulture and Oenology from Lincoln University. She relocated permanently to Australia in 2019 and has completed 11 Australian vintages, alongside Northern Hemisphere experience in Canada and France, as well as vintages in her native New Zealand. She completed a Diploma of Business Studies through Massey University in 2022, the Australian Wine Industry Advanced Course (AWAC) in 2024, and is currently undertaking her WSET Diploma, having completed WSET Level 3 in 2025.

Wallace is actively involved in wine judging across Australia, including having served at the Adelaide Royal and Melbourne Royal Wine Shows as an associate judge. Moreover, she is a long-standing member of the Clare Valley Wine Show Committee.

content:
Sydney Royal Wine Show chair of judges Mike de Iuliis. Image supplied

The Royal Agricultural Society of NSW (RAS) invites Australian wine producers to enter the Sydney Royal Wine Show for its 200th anniversary.

Throughout its history, the RAS said that the show has been guided by some of Australian wine’s greatest figures, including Len Evans AO, James Halliday AM, Brian Croser AO, Iain Riggs AM, Samantha Connew, PJ Charteris and Sarah Crowe.

This year’s show will be led by chair of judges Mike de Iuliis, who is joined by international judge Matthew Jukes. The RAS emphasised that a Sydney Royal medal is “one of the most recognised and trusted marks in Australian wine”, with a milestone year such as this one carrying “more weight than ever”.

Chair of wine committee Angus Barnes said that the show has been setting the benchmark for Australian wine quality since 1826.

“The show has kept pace with every shift in Australian wine — from fortified styles to the classic varietal wines Australia is celebrated for globally, through to the emerging styles reflecting how Australian drinking continues to evolve.

“With nearly 2,000 wines entered on average each year, being part of Sydney Royal means being part of the institution that has helped showcase the best of Australian wine to the world for two centuries,” Barnes said.

Entries cost $150 per exhibit and close 20 May.

The RAS is also proud to announce an industry symposium titled the ‘Sydney Royal 200: The Wine Show Conversation’, with priority access for tickets to be given to Sydney Royal Wine Show entrants.

The symposium will immediately follow judging on 31 July, with 200 places available for wine professionals “to explore the Australian wine show system and go behind the scenes to see how wine is assessed”. RAS said the day will be full of tasting, debate and celebration of Australian wine. General ticket sales opened last Friday.

“Over 200 years it has been a constant theme of cherishing all that is great about the past, while always having the future front of mind, embracing innovation, fostering youth and nurturing communities as we move collectively into the next generation,” said Sally Evans, Wine committee member and RAS councillor.

content:
Nicole Brasier, Bleasdale senior winemaker. Image supplied

Langhorne Creek grapegrowers, wineries, trade, suppliers and representatives from local and state government agencies came together last Friday to celebrate the 2026 Langhorne Creek Wine Show.

Bleasdale winery saw much success at the ceremony, winning the ‘Giles Machinery Best Malbec’ trophy and the ‘Multi-Color Corporation Champion Wine of Show’ award for its Bleasdale Reed Patch 2025 Single Vineyard Malbec before being announced as the ‘Vinpac Producer of the Year’.

Watkins Wine vineyard manager Mark Evans was named the ‘Peats Soil & Garden Supplies Grape Grower of the Year’ for “his longstanding contribution to the Langhorne Creek region and community”.

Other awards saw a variety of producers acknowledged, with trophies going to the Bremerton 2025 Special Release Fiano, the W Salter & Son 2025 Pepperjack Grenache Rosé, the Wolf Blass 2024 Grey Label Cabernet Shiraz, the Lake Breeze Wines 2024 Cabernet Sauvignon, the Kimbolton 2021 The Rifleman Shiraz, the Lake Breeze 2025 Nero D’Avola and the Lake Breeze Wines 2016 Arthur’s Reserve Cabernet Sauvignon.

Chair of judges Adam Walls (Wine Selectors) was joined by judges Alexandra Alavoine (Treasury Wine Estates) and wine communicator Samantha Payne, as well as associate judges Charlotte Hardy (Charlotte Dalton Wines) and Gonzalo Sanchez (Lloyd Brothers).

In his first year as chair, Walls was impressed by Langhorne Creek’s diversity.

“The show was an insightful immersion into the region’s wines, with an impressively high standard of winemaking demonstrated across all classes.

“The red categories revealed an encouraging trend toward fresher, more vibrant expressions, complemented by thoughtful and well-integrated oak usage.

“The alternative red and white classes further highlighted the region’s versatility, showcasing that its strengths extend well beyond the traditional stalwarts of Cabernet Sauvignon and Shiraz.

“The Malbec class was especially noteworthy. The 20 wines presented demonstrated remarkable diversity in vineyard origin, clonal selection and house style, reinforcing the view that Langhorne Creek comfortably sits at the top of this variety’s totem pole,” he said.

Walls awarded the ‘Chair of Judges trophy’ to the Terrachroma Wines 2025 Long Shot Greco.

“Not only was this wine aromatic and full of flavour, [but] it also had textural weight and chew. The role of texture and mouthfeel in Australian white wine has become more considered of late, more discussed and celebrated. This wine perfectly captured our maturity in how we look at white wines.”

Since 1985, Penfolds has opened its cellars to an invited panel of independent, highly acclaimed wine critics from around the globe to taste and review its archive of wines. The insights, tasting notes and perspectives of these critics is the published into Rewards of Patience alongside the latest research and the stories behind each Penfolds wine. The post Penfolds commemorates 40 years of wine guide with 9th edition appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
17 wine critics from Hong Kong, London, Paris and Adelaide contributed to the guide. Image supplied

Penfolds has released the 9th edition of its Rewards of Patience back-vintage wine guide, marking 40 years “of one of the most respected wine publications in the world”.

Since 1985, Penfolds has opened its cellars to an invited panel of independent, highly acclaimed wine critics from around the globe to taste and review its archive of wines. The insights, tasting notes and perspectives of these critics is the published into Rewards of Patience alongside the latest research and the stories behind each Penfolds wine.

Compiled and chronicled by independent author Andrew Caillard MW, the book is affectionately known as the Penfolds “Form Guide” and “widely regarded as the definitive reference for all things Penfolds”.

Over a six-month period, a panel of 17 wine critics came together across Hong Kong, London, Paris and Adelaide to taste more than 500 wines from Penfolds Museum. Grange, Bin 707 and Yattarna led the tasting, however, the panel were also shown some of the winery’s “most rare and limited releases”, including Bin 60A Coonawarra Cabernet, Kalimna Vineyard Block 42 Cabernet Sauvignon and the Penfolds g Series.

“People from all walks of life have followed Penfolds for generations. It is one of the most collected Australian wine brands in the world and has a storied track record in the secondary markets. A complete collection of Grange and rare bottlings can command eye-watering auction prices, while regular trading in more recent vintages highlights its popularity and dependability.

“In addition, it is one of the most enjoyable, versatile and trusted wine styles in the world, especially with a few years or more of bottle age. Hence why Penfolds appeals to such a varied audience, in so many countries,” author Caillard said.

Chief winemaker for Penfolds Peter Gago AC, said that each edition of the wine guide “ascends to a new level”.

“As wine drinkers and collectors, we all need guidance and reference. This tome supports our search, and answers many of our questions – honestly, and as accurately as can be expected with ever-morphing wines and their subjective and emotive appreciation. It’s certainly an honour for the Penfolds winemaking team to remain intimately involved in such a project and such a pursuit,” he said.

The winery said that in addition to Penfolds Australian wines, the 9th edition also spotlights the brand’s expanding international portfolio, including wines from California, France and China with over 500 tasting notes inside the book.

The book is available for $50 RRP as a hardback copy or $29 for a paperback via the Penfolds website.

title: Four Winds Vineyard makes Australia’s first ‘circular wine’ using black soldier fly larvae
summary:
Four Winds Vineyard has released Australia’s first ‘circular wine’ in collaboration with hotelier Hyatt Regency Sydney and food waste innovator Goterra using millions of fly larvae. The initiative sees food waste from the Hyatt Regency consumed by the larvae and recycled onsite into a rich frass, or fertiliser, which nourishes the grapevines that are used to produce Four Winds Vineyard’s new ‘The Circular Vintage’ series. “In the circular production process, organic waste from the Hyatt’s kitchens goes directly into Goterra’s specially built Modular Infrastructure for Biological Services (MIB) unit in the hotel’s basement. Resembling a shipping container the size of a single car space, the MIB is filled with millions of black soldier fly larvae that eat their way through the food scraps, excreting nutrient-rich frass,” Four Winds Vineyard explained.

The post Four Winds Vineyard makes Australia’s first ‘circular wine’ using black soldier fly larvae appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The ‘frass’ used to nourish the grapevines. Image supplied

Four Winds Vineyard has released Australia’s first ‘circular wine’ in collaboration with hotelier Hyatt Regency Sydney and food waste innovator Goterra using millions of fly larvae.

The initiative sees food waste from the Hyatt Regency consumed by the larvae and recycled onsite into a rich frass, or fertiliser, which nourishes the grapevines that are used to produce Four Winds Vineyard’s new ‘The Circular Vintage’ series.

“In the circular production process, organic waste from the Hyatt’s kitchens goes directly into Goterra’s specially built Modular Infrastructure for Biological Services (MIB) unit in the hotel’s basement. Resembling a shipping container the size of a single car space, the MIB is filled with millions of black soldier fly larvae that eat their way through the food scraps, excreting nutrient-rich frass.

“The fertiliser is used on Four Winds Vineyard’s vines while the maggots, having doubled their size daily during a seven-day feeding frenzy, are used as protein for poultry and pet food,” Four Winds Vineyard explained.

Four Winds Vineyard CEO Sarah Collingwood said the circular wine project made perfect sense for the family-run vineyard.

“We loved the idea of being able to return nutrients to the soil rather than having them end up in landfill.

“For us, this was about finding a practical way to close the loop between food, farming and wine, and we’re incredibly proud to put our name to The Circular Vintage series, which shows that innovation and quality can go hand in hand.

“The Circular Vintage Shiraz is a typical cool-climate Shiraz with distinctive white pepper and spice characters. We are equally excited about the Riesling, which has been hand-picked recently and has the delicate citrus and floral flavours you come to expect from a Canberra District Riesling,” Collingwood said.

Goterra CEO Olympia Yarger, who originally partnered with Hyatt Regency Sydney in 2024 to install the robot-controlled maggot farm as a way of sustainably managing the hotel’s food waste, said the beauty of the process is that nothing is wasted.

“This is the first time our system has been used to support wine production.

“Using insect frass to nourish vines creates a solution that benefits the vineyard, the hotel and the environment, and opens the door for new thinking across the wine industry,” she said.

Hyatt Regency Sydney executive assistant manager Nitin Kumar said Four Winds Vineyard stood out as a producer that shared the hotel’s commitment to quality and innovation.

“Partnering with Four Winds Vineyard on an Australia-first initiative like this allows us to showcase wines with a story that resonates well beyond the glass,” he said.

The Four Winds Vineyard 2025 Circular Vintage Shiraz is being poured for the first time today in the Hyatt Regency’s restaurant and lounge bar. A Circular Vintage Riesling will be introduced at the hotel in September.

title: The Islander Estate Vineyards achieves sustainability certification across its operations
summary:
The Islander Estate Vineyards has received certification from Sustainable Winegrowing Australia (SWA), an achievement the winery said strengthens its commitment to environmentally conscious viticulture and winemaking. The new certification covers Islander Estate Vineyards’ four Kangaroo Island vineyards and winery operations. The estate said that it has farmed without synthetic pesticides, herbicides, growth regulators, or GMOs for 15 years. Yale Norris, co-owner of Islander Estate Vineyards, said that its isolated location and existing growing principals made the winery a natural candidate for the SWA certification. “We haven't had to make significant changes to the way we grow or make wine. The certification is our commitment to ongoing improvements in sustainability that are independently audited by SWA, giving our customers the reassurance that we are doing our utmost to protect both this land and the wines we produce from it,” he explained.

The post The Islander Estate Vineyards achieves sustainability certification across its operations appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The certification covers all four vineyards and winery operations. Image supplied

The Islander Estate Vineyards has received certification from Sustainable Winegrowing Australia (SWA), an achievement the winery said strengthens its commitment to environmentally conscious viticulture and winemaking.

The new certification covers The Islander Estate Vineyards’ four Kangaroo Island vineyards and winery operations. The estate said that it has farmed without synthetic pesticides, herbicides, growth regulators, or GMOs for 15 years.

Yale Norris, co-owner of The Islander Estate Vineyards, said that its isolated location and existing growing principals made the winery a natural candidate for the SWA certification.

“We haven’t had to make significant changes to the way we grow or make wine. The certification is our commitment to ongoing improvements in sustainability that are independently audited by SWA, giving our customers the reassurance that we are doing our utmost to protect both this land and the wines we produce from it,” he explained.

Norris said that the breadth of the SWA program is a key part of its appeal.

“We were attracted to SWA because while environmental management is a key pillar, it encompasses social and economic performance alongside environmental stewardship, and the program is about continuous improvement. It sets the vision for where we want to be, not just confirms where we already are.”

The winery explained that SWA is the national certification program for Australian grapegrowers and winemakers, backed by the Australian Wine Research Institute, Wine Australia, and Australian Grape & Wine.

Certification takes a “holistic view” of sustainability across six key pillars: Land & Soil, Water, People & Business, Biodiversity, Energy and Waste. It requires regular independent audits and a commitment to ongoing measurement and improvement.

The post Sevenhill names just its 10th chief winemaker as it marks 175 years appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Ben Marx, the newly appointed chief winemaker. Image supplied

Sevenhill Cellars in the Clare Valley has appointed just its 10th chief winemaker and its third-non-Jesuit winemaker in its 175-year history.

Ben Marx will be joining Sevenhill following significant winemaking roles at Knappstein and Jim Barry Wines. The Jesuits and the board of Sevenhill Cellars said Marx brings to the role “a deep understanding of Clare’s viticultural landscape, a firm grounding in its traditions and a clear vision for its stylistic evolution”.

Marx expressed his excitement to be joining the team.

“After seven years at Jim Barry’s I am more than excited to join the team at Sevenhill Cellars – a new challenge, a fruit resource second to none and a team committed to not only be around for another 175 years but also to make wine of global benchmark quality.

“With some major developments happening over the next few years and the commitment of the Jesuits, I can’t wait to get started,” he said.

“The Barry family, particularly my late grandparents, Jim and Nancy, has a long association with Sevenhill Cellars and the Jesuit community, with mutual support at the heart of that relationship in both winemaking and in faith. We wish Ben and the Sevenhill team all the best as they embark on this new chapter,” said Tom Barry of Jim Barry Wines.

Fr Brendan J Kelly SJ, Superior of Sevenhill from the Jesuits community, said that the mantra of the first Jesuit to Sevenhill was “courage, patience and hope”.

“We are very excited at the appointment of Ben Marx as chief winemaker, joining our general manager, Christian Fraser, and viticulturist, Craig Richards, forming a formidable team to continue the tradition at Sevenhill of quality wine for over 175 years,” he said.

Founded in 1851, Sevenhill is the Clare Valley’s foundational winery, and, for its first 150 years, winemaking was conducted by Jesuits.

title: Barossa InterContinental hotel development green lit
summary:
A new hotel and tourism development has been granted planning approval in the Barossa Valley. The SA state government said the development is “paving the way for a world-class hospitality offering in one of South Australia's most celebrated wine regions”. Strategic Alliance and Commercial Pty Ltd has been granted approval by the Minister for Planning to develop the 5-star hotel and winery on Hoffnungsthal Road, Williamstown. The government claims that once operating, the InterContinental Barossa Resort & Spa will generate $70-$100 million in annual economic benefits to the Barossa. The project will deliver up to 150 hotel rooms overlooking the wine region, a restaurant and “premium function facilities”.

The post Barossa InterContinental hotel development green lit appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Artist impression of the future Barossa InterContinental. Image supplied

A new hotel and tourism development has been granted planning approval in the Barossa Valley.

The SA state government said the development is “paving the way for a world-class hospitality offering in one of South Australia’s most celebrated wine regions”.

Strategic Alliance and Commercial Pty Ltd has been granted approval by the Minister for Planning to develop the 5-star hotel and winery on Hoffnungsthal Road, Williamstown.

The government claims that once operating, the InterContinental Barossa Resort & Spa will generate $70-$100 million in annual economic benefits to the Barossa.

The project will deliver up to 150 hotel rooms overlooking the wine region, a restaurant and “premium function facilities”.

The proposal was declared a ‘Major Project’ on 8 August 2024 and classified as an ‘Impact Assessed Development’.

The government said this process required the preparation of a detailed ‘Environmental Impact Statement’ (EIS), with assessment requirements set by the independent State Planning Commission.

The EIS was made available for community feedback, which raised key concerns such as landscape and character, traffic and congestion, bushfire risk, as well as infrastructure impacts and design. These concerns were then “thoroughly considered and addressed as part of the assessment process”.

Minister for Housing and Urban Development MP Nick Champion said the approval has been earned “through the most stringent assessment process available” in SA.

“Community voices were heard, key issues were carefully examined, and independent expert advice was considered every step of the way.

“This is the right outcome for the Barossa and South Australia.

“This development is a vote of confidence in South Australia, and the economic benefits will flow through the Barossa for generations to come,” he said.

Minister for Tourism Emily Bourke MLC said the development would be an exciting addition to the state’s accommodation sector.

“This is another example of the growing confidence in South Australia as not only a global destination of choice but as an attractive place to invest and do business.

“We are experiencing record growth in the accommodation sector, supported by our investment in major events and strong tourism offerings,” she said.

Directors at Strategic Alliance, George Economou and David Cook, shared that they are pleased to have received planning approval.

“Our team is committed to delivering a high‑quality hotel that contributes to South Australia’s tourism offering and supports local jobs.

“We are also grateful for the constructive engagement with various state government and regional agencies throughout the planning pathway and take this opportunity to thank them, our project team and everyone we have engaged with on this journey.”

title: China to host the 47th World Congress of Vine and Wine
summary:
From 12 to 16 October 2026, the 47th World Congress of Vine and Wine alongside the 24th General Assembly of the International Organisation of Vine and Wine (OIV) will take place in the city of Yinchuan, Ningxia (PR China). Under the theme of ‘shaping new paradigms for the global vine and wine sector: transforming to meet challenges, innovating to ignite vitality, and sustaining to anchor a shared future’, the OIV said Congress will bring together leading international experts to address the major scientific, technical and economic challenges facing the sector. The OIV is inviting researchers and professionals worldwide to submit abstracts across four key sessions.

content:
China will host in October this year. Image supplied

From 12 to 16 October 2026, the 47th World Congress of Vine and Wine alongside the 24th General Assembly of the International Organisation of Vine and Wine (OIV) will take place in the city of Yinchuan, Ningxia (PR China).

Under the theme of ‘shaping new paradigms for the global vine and wine sector: transforming to meet challenges, innovating to ignite vitality, and sustaining to anchor a shared future’, the OIV said Congress will bring together leading international experts to address the major scientific, technical and economic challenges facing the sector.

The OIV is inviting researchers and professionals worldwide to submit abstracts across four key sessions:

  • Viticulture: Integrated approaches to resilient and sustainable grape production
  • Oenology: Technical innovation and adapt oenology to the future of wine
  • Economy & Law: Resilient legal and economic models in a changing market
  • Safety & Health: Better production, better consumption — health and social sustainability at the heart of the vitivinicultural chain.

“This is a unique opportunity to engage with world-leading specialists in viticulture, oenology, health, safety, law and economics within the global vine and wine sector,” the OIV said.

The deadline for abstract submissions is 31 May 2026, whilst final paper submissions (accepted presentations) are due 30 September 2026.

Any questions about submitting presentations should be submitted to papers@oiv.int

Jim Barry Wines explained that she consistently demonstrates exceptional technical skill, leadership, sound judgement, and a deep understanding of the brand’s philosophy. Director of winemaking Tom Barry said that Wallace has “more than earned this role”. “Her work ethic, attention to detail, and ability to lead with confidence and clarity have been evident for years. This appointment is a natural progression based on merit, experience, and results,” he said.

content:
Newly appointed senior winemaker, Topsi Wallace. Image supplied

Jim Barry Wines has announced the appointment of Topsi Wallace as its senior winemaker, making her the first female to hold the position in its 67 years of operation.

The winery said that the appointment recognises her six years of “outstanding contribution” to the family-owned winery and the “pivotal role” she has played across all aspects of winemaking.

Jim Barry Wines explained that she consistently demonstrates exceptional technical skill, leadership, sound judgement, and a deep understanding of the brand’s philosophy.

Director of winemaking Tom Barry said that Wallace has “more than earned this role”.

“Her work ethic, attention to detail, and ability to lead with confidence and clarity have been evident for years. This appointment is a natural progression based on merit, experience, and results,” he said.

Although the appointment of its first female senior winemaker is a milestone, Jim Barry Wines emphasised that the outcome is “of proven capability and sustained performance”.

“Topsi’s role has been earned through hard work, expertise, and leadership. She has our complete trust and respect,” said Barry.

In the last four years, Jim Barry Wines has achieved 206 gold medals and 64 trophies at Australian Wine Shows, making it the most awarded Riesling and Shiraz producer in the country.

“Topsi has played a central role in achieving these outstanding results, of which we are incredibly proud.”

Jim Barry Wines said that senior winemaker is the highest winemaking position at Jim Barry Wines, outside of the Barry family, and “carries responsibility for overseeing production, upholding quality standards across the portfolio, and mentoring the wider winemaking team”.

Originally from New Zealand’s South Island, Wallace holds a Bachelor of Viticulture and Oenology from Lincoln University. She relocated permanently to Australia in 2019 and has completed 11 Australian vintages, alongside Northern Hemisphere experience in Canada and France, as well as vintages in her native New Zealand. She completed a Diploma of Business Studies through Massey University in 2022, the Australian Wine Industry Advanced Course (AWAC) in 2024, and is currently undertaking her WSET Diploma, having completed WSET Level 3 in 2025.

Wallace is actively involved in wine judging across Australia, including having served at the Adelaide Royal and Melbourne Royal Wine Shows as an associate judge. Moreover, she is a long-standing member of the Clare Valley Wine Show Committee.

content:
Last year’s tutorials in Melbourne (pictured) and Sydney were a success. Image supplied

The Coonawarra Vignerons has revealed that it will be bringing its ‘Coonawarra Tutorial: Digging Deeper than the Dirt’ to Adelaide and Brisbane this June following last year’s success in Melbourne and Sydney.

The tutorial aims to showcase the past, present and future of the wine region “going beyond the soil” to explore viticulture and the evolving styles of Coonawarra wines.

The events will take place in Adelaide on Wednesday 3 June and Brisbane Wednesday 10 June, “bringing together leading sommeliers, wine trade, and media for an engaging and educational tasting experience”.

A select group of representatives will be given the opportunity to hear panel members Dan Redman (Redman Wines), Matilda Innes (Ottelia), Maddie LeMire (Balnaves of Coonawarra) and Ben Harris (Wynns Coonawarra Estate), present a range of wines dating back to the 1960s with discussion facilitated by wine educator Sarah Andrew (DipWSET Honours).

With a population of just 135 people, the organisation said the region’s strength lies in “community spirit, collaboration, innovation, and a shared vision to keep Coonawarra wines relevant for future generations”.

This year’s show will be led by chair of judges Mike de Iuliis, who is joined by international judge Matthew Jukes. content:
Sydney Royal Wine Show chair of judges Mike de Iuliis. Image supplied

The Royal Agricultural Society of NSW (RAS) invites Australian wine producers to enter the Sydney Royal Wine Show for its 200th anniversary.

Throughout its history, the RAS said that the show has been guided by some of Australian wine’s greatest figures, including Len Evans AO, James Halliday AM, Brian Croser AO, Iain Riggs AM, Samantha Connew, PJ Charteris and Sarah Crowe.

This year’s show will be led by chair of judges Mike de Iuliis, who is joined by international judge Matthew Jukes. The RAS emphasised that a Sydney Royal medal is “one of the most recognised and trusted marks in Australian wine”, with a milestone year such as this one carrying “more weight than ever”.

Chair of wine committee Angus Barnes said that the show has been setting the benchmark for Australian wine quality since 1826.

“The show has kept pace with every shift in Australian wine — from fortified styles to the classic varietal wines Australia is celebrated for globally, through to the emerging styles reflecting how Australian drinking continues to evolve.

“With nearly 2,000 wines entered on average each year, being part of Sydney Royal means being part of the institution that has helped showcase the best of Australian wine to the world for two centuries,” Barnes said.

Entries cost $150 per exhibit and close 20 May.

The RAS is also proud to announce an industry symposium titled the ‘Sydney Royal 200: The Wine Show Conversation’, with priority access for tickets to be given to Sydney Royal Wine Show entrants.

The symposium will immediately follow judging on 31 July, with 200 places available for wine professionals “to explore the Australian wine show system and go behind the scenes to see how wine is assessed”. RAS said the day will be full of tasting, debate and celebration of Australian wine. General ticket sales opened last Friday.

“Over 200 years it has been a constant theme of cherishing all that is great about the past, while always having the future front of mind, embracing innovation, fostering youth and nurturing communities as we move collectively into the next generation,” said Sally Evans, Wine committee member and RAS councillor.

content:
Nicole Brasier, Bleasdale senior winemaker. Image supplied

Langhorne Creek grapegrowers, wineries, trade, suppliers and representatives from local and state government agencies came together last Friday to celebrate the 2026 Langhorne Creek Wine Show.

Bleasdale winery saw much success at the ceremony, winning the ‘Giles Machinery Best Malbec’ trophy and the ‘Multi-Color Corporation Champion Wine of Show’ award for its Bleasdale Reed Patch 2025 Single Vineyard Malbec before being announced as the ‘Vinpac Producer of the Year’.

Watkins Wine vineyard manager Mark Evans was named the ‘Peats Soil & Garden Supplies Grape Grower of the Year’ for “his longstanding contribution to the Langhorne Creek region and community”.

Other awards saw a variety of producers acknowledged, with trophies going to the Bremerton 2025 Special Release Fiano, the W Salter & Son 2025 Pepperjack Grenache Rosé, the Wolf Blass 2024 Grey Label Cabernet Shiraz, the Lake Breeze Wines 2024 Cabernet Sauvignon, the Kimbolton 2021 The Rifleman Shiraz, the Lake Breeze 2025 Nero D’Avola and the Lake Breeze Wines 2016 Arthur’s Reserve Cabernet Sauvignon.

Chair of judges Adam Walls (Wine Selectors) was joined by judges Alexandra Alavoine (Treasury Wine Estates) and wine communicator Samantha Payne, as well as associate judges Charlotte Hardy (Charlotte Dalton Wines) and Gonzalo Sanchez (Lloyd Brothers).

In his first year as chair, Walls was impressed by Langhorne Creek’s diversity.

“The show was an insightful immersion into the region’s wines, with an impressively high standard of winemaking demonstrated across all classes.

“The red categories revealed an encouraging trend toward fresher, more vibrant expressions, complemented by thoughtful and well-integrated oak usage.

“The alternative red and white classes further highlighted the region’s versatility, showcasing that its strengths extend well beyond the traditional stalwarts of Cabernet Sauvignon and Shiraz.

“The Malbec class was especially noteworthy. The 20 wines presented demonstrated remarkable diversity in vineyard origin, clonal selection and house style, reinforcing the view that Langhorne Creek comfortably sits at the top of this variety’s totem pole,” he said.

Walls awarded the ‘Chair of Judges trophy’ to the Terrachroma Wines 2025 Long Shot Greco.

“Not only was this wine aromatic and full of flavour, [but] it also had textural weight and chew. The role of texture and mouthfeel in Australian white wine has become more considered of late, more discussed and celebrated. This wine perfectly captured our maturity in how we look at white wines.”

summary:

The insights, tasting notes and perspectives of these critics is the published into Rewards of Patience alongside the latest research and the stories behind each Penfolds wine. The post Penfolds commemorates 40 years of wine guide with 9th edition appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
17 wine critics from Hong Kong, London, Paris and Adelaide contributed to the guide. Image supplied

Penfolds has released the 9th edition of its Rewards of Patience back-vintage wine guide, marking 40 years “of one of the most respected wine publications in the world”.

Since 1985, Penfolds has opened its cellars to an invited panel of independent, highly acclaimed wine critics from around the globe to taste and review its archive of wines. The insights, tasting notes and perspectives of these critics is the published into Rewards of Patience alongside the latest research and the stories behind each Penfolds wine.

Compiled and chronicled by independent author Andrew Caillard MW, the book is affectionately known as the Penfolds “Form Guide” and “widely regarded as the definitive reference for all things Penfolds”.

Over a six-month period, a panel of 17 wine critics came together across Hong Kong, London, Paris and Adelaide to taste more than 500 wines from Penfolds Museum. Grange, Bin 707 and Yattarna led the tasting, however, the panel were also shown some of the winery’s “most rare and limited releases”, including Bin 60A Coonawarra Cabernet, Kalimna Vineyard Block 42 Cabernet Sauvignon and the Penfolds g Series.

“People from all walks of life have followed Penfolds for generations. It is one of the most collected Australian wine brands in the world and has a storied track record in the secondary markets. A complete collection of Grange and rare bottlings can command eye-watering auction prices, while regular trading in more recent vintages highlights its popularity and dependability.

“In addition, it is one of the most enjoyable, versatile and trusted wine styles in the world, especially with a few years or more of bottle age. Hence why Penfolds appeals to such a varied audience, in so many countries,” author Caillard said.

Chief winemaker for Penfolds Peter Gago AC, said that each edition of the wine guide “ascends to a new level”.

“As wine drinkers and collectors, we all need guidance and reference. This tome supports our search, and answers many of our questions – honestly, and as accurately as can be expected with ever-morphing wines and their subjective and emotive appreciation. It’s certainly an honour for the Penfolds winemaking team to remain intimately involved in such a project and such a pursuit,” he said.

The winery said that in addition to Penfolds Australian wines, the 9th edition also spotlights the brand’s expanding international portfolio, including wines from California, France and China with over 500 tasting notes inside the book.

The book is available for $50 RRP as a hardback copy or $29 for a paperback via the Penfolds website.

title: Four Winds Vineyard makes Australia’s first ‘circular wine’ using black soldier fly larvae
summary:
Four Winds Vineyard has released Australia’s first ‘circular wine’ in collaboration with hotelier Hyatt Regency Sydney and food waste innovator Goterra using millions of fly larvae. The initiative sees food waste from the Hyatt Regency consumed by the larvae and recycled onsite into a rich frass, or fertiliser, which nourishes the grapevines that are used to produce Four Winds Vineyard’s new ‘The Circular Vintage’ series. “In the circular production process, organic waste from the Hyatt’s kitchens goes directly into Goterra’s specially built Modular Infrastructure for Biological Services (MIB) unit in the hotel’s basement. Resembling a shipping container the size of a single car space, the MIB is filled with millions of black soldier fly larvae that eat their way through the food scraps, excreting nutrient-rich frass,” Four Winds Vineyard explained.

The post Four Winds Vineyard makes Australia’s first ‘circular wine’ using black soldier fly larvae appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The ‘frass’ used to nourish the grapevines. Image supplied

Four Winds Vineyard has released Australia’s first ‘circular wine’ in collaboration with hotelier Hyatt Regency Sydney and food waste innovator Goterra using millions of fly larvae.

The initiative sees food waste from the Hyatt Regency consumed by the larvae and recycled onsite into a rich frass, or fertiliser, which nourishes the grapevines that are used to produce Four Winds Vineyard’s new ‘The Circular Vintage’ series.

“In the circular production process, organic waste from the Hyatt’s kitchens goes directly into Goterra’s specially built Modular Infrastructure for Biological Services (MIB) unit in the hotel’s basement. Resembling a shipping container the size of a single car space, the MIB is filled with millions of black soldier fly larvae that eat their way through the food scraps, excreting nutrient-rich frass.

“The fertiliser is used on Four Winds Vineyard’s vines while the maggots, having doubled their size daily during a seven-day feeding frenzy, are used as protein for poultry and pet food,” Four Winds Vineyard explained.

Four Winds Vineyard CEO Sarah Collingwood said the circular wine project made perfect sense for the family-run vineyard.

“We loved the idea of being able to return nutrients to the soil rather than having them end up in landfill.

“For us, this was about finding a practical way to close the loop between food, farming and wine, and we’re incredibly proud to put our name to The Circular Vintage series, which shows that innovation and quality can go hand in hand.

“The Circular Vintage Shiraz is a typical cool-climate Shiraz with distinctive white pepper and spice characters. We are equally excited about the Riesling, which has been hand-picked recently and has the delicate citrus and floral flavours you come to expect from a Canberra District Riesling,” Collingwood said.

Goterra CEO Olympia Yarger, who originally partnered with Hyatt Regency Sydney in 2024 to install the robot-controlled maggot farm as a way of sustainably managing the hotel’s food waste, said the beauty of the process is that nothing is wasted.

“This is the first time our system has been used to support wine production.

“Using insect frass to nourish vines creates a solution that benefits the vineyard, the hotel and the environment, and opens the door for new thinking across the wine industry,” she said.

Hyatt Regency Sydney executive assistant manager Nitin Kumar said Four Winds Vineyard stood out as a producer that shared the hotel’s commitment to quality and innovation.

“Partnering with Four Winds Vineyard on an Australia-first initiative like this allows us to showcase wines with a story that resonates well beyond the glass,” he said.

The Four Winds Vineyard 2025 Circular Vintage Shiraz is being poured for the first time today in the Hyatt Regency’s restaurant and lounge bar. A Circular Vintage Riesling will be introduced at the hotel in September.

title: Barossa InterContinental hotel development green lit
summary:
A new hotel and tourism development has been granted planning approval in the Barossa Valley. The SA state government said the development is “paving the way for a world-class hospitality offering in one of South Australia's most celebrated wine regions”. Strategic Alliance and Commercial Pty Ltd has been granted approval by the Minister for Planning to develop the 5-star hotel and winery on Hoffnungsthal Road, Williamstown. The government claims that once operating, the InterContinental Barossa Resort & Spa will generate $70-$100 million in annual economic benefits to the Barossa. The project will deliver up to 150 hotel rooms overlooking the wine region, a restaurant and “premium function facilities”.

The post Barossa InterContinental hotel development green lit appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Artist impression of the future Barossa InterContinental. Image supplied

A new hotel and tourism development has been granted planning approval in the Barossa Valley.

The SA state government said the development is “paving the way for a world-class hospitality offering in one of South Australia’s most celebrated wine regions”.

Strategic Alliance and Commercial Pty Ltd has been granted approval by the Minister for Planning to develop the 5-star hotel and winery on Hoffnungsthal Road, Williamstown.

The government claims that once operating, the InterContinental Barossa Resort & Spa will generate $70-$100 million in annual economic benefits to the Barossa.

The project will deliver up to 150 hotel rooms overlooking the wine region, a restaurant and “premium function facilities”.

The proposal was declared a ‘Major Project’ on 8 August 2024 and classified as an ‘Impact Assessed Development’.

The government said this process required the preparation of a detailed ‘Environmental Impact Statement’ (EIS), with assessment requirements set by the independent State Planning Commission.

The EIS was made available for community feedback, which raised key concerns such as landscape and character, traffic and congestion, bushfire risk, as well as infrastructure impacts and design. These concerns were then “thoroughly considered and addressed as part of the assessment process”.

Minister for Housing and Urban Development MP Nick Champion said the approval has been earned “through the most stringent assessment process available” in SA.

“Community voices were heard, key issues were carefully examined, and independent expert advice was considered every step of the way.

“This is the right outcome for the Barossa and South Australia.

“This development is a vote of confidence in South Australia, and the economic benefits will flow through the Barossa for generations to come,” he said.

Minister for Tourism Emily Bourke MLC said the development would be an exciting addition to the state’s accommodation sector.

“This is another example of the growing confidence in South Australia as not only a global destination of choice but as an attractive place to invest and do business.

“We are experiencing record growth in the accommodation sector, supported by our investment in major events and strong tourism offerings,” she said.

Directors at Strategic Alliance, George Economou and David Cook, shared that they are pleased to have received planning approval.

“Our team is committed to delivering a high‑quality hotel that contributes to South Australia’s tourism offering and supports local jobs.

“We are also grateful for the constructive engagement with various state government and regional agencies throughout the planning pathway and take this opportunity to thank them, our project team and everyone we have engaged with on this journey.”

summary:
From 12 to 16 October 2026, the 47th World Congress of Vine and Wine alongside the 24th General Assembly of the International Organisation of Vine and Wine (OIV) will take place in the city of Yinchuan, Ningxia (PR China). Under the theme of ‘shaping new paradigms for the global vine and wine sector: transforming to meet challenges, innovating to ignite vitality, and sustaining to anchor a shared future’, the OIV said Congress will bring together leading international experts to address the major scientific, technical and economic challenges facing the sector. The OIV is inviting researchers and professionals worldwide to submit abstracts across four key sessions.

content:
China will host in October this year. Image supplied

From 12 to 16 October 2026, the 47th World Congress of Vine and Wine alongside the 24th General Assembly of the International Organisation of Vine and Wine (OIV) will take place in the city of Yinchuan, Ningxia (PR China).

Under the theme of ‘shaping new paradigms for the global vine and wine sector: transforming to meet challenges, innovating to ignite vitality, and sustaining to anchor a shared future’, the OIV said Congress will bring together leading international experts to address the major scientific, technical and economic challenges facing the sector.

The OIV is inviting researchers and professionals worldwide to submit abstracts across four key sessions:

  • Viticulture: Integrated approaches to resilient and sustainable grape production
  • Oenology: Technical innovation and adapt oenology to the future of wine
  • Economy & Law: Resilient legal and economic models in a changing market
  • Safety & Health: Better production, better consumption — health and social sustainability at the heart of the vitivinicultural chain.

“This is a unique opportunity to engage with world-leading specialists in viticulture, oenology, health, safety, law and economics within the global vine and wine sector,” the OIV said.

The deadline for abstract submissions is 31 May 2026, whilst final paper submissions (accepted presentations) are due 30 September 2026.

Any questions about submitting presentations should be submitted to papers@oiv.int

Jim Barry Wines explained that she consistently demonstrates exceptional technical skill, leadership, sound judgement, and a deep understanding of the brand’s philosophy. Director of winemaking Tom Barry said that Wallace has “more than earned this role”. “Her work ethic, attention to detail, and ability to lead with confidence and clarity have been evident for years. This appointment is a natural progression based on merit, experience, and results,” he said.
